summary: Wave, a $1.7B fintech company with 70 engineers, deliberately uses a simple Python monolith architecture instead of trendy microservices. The author argues that for most applications, even at massive scale, simple architectures are more cost-effective and allow engineers to focus on user value rather than managing architectural complexity. Despite the tech industry's obsession with sophisticated architectures, companies like Wave and Stack Overflow have scaled to billions in value using boring, monolithic approaches.
key_points: Wave serves billions of requests monthly with a simple Python/Postgres monolith, proving simple architectures work at scale
The cost of engineering teams dominates system costs for most businesses, making expensive but complex optimizations unnecessary
Tech conferences heavily favor complex microservice architectures despite monoliths being effective for most use cases
Complexity should only be added where it provides clear business value, not because it's fashionable
Some complexity is unavoidable (like data residency requirements and telecom integrations in Africa), so simplicity elsewhere is crucial
money_quotes: Wave is a $1.7B company with 70 engineers whose product is a CRUD app that adds and subtracts numbers.
The cost of our engineering team completely dominates the cost of the systems we operate.
There were more talks on quantum computing (one) than talks on monoliths (zero).
Computers are fast enough that high-traffic apps can be served with simple architectures, which can generally be created more cheaply and easily than complex architectures.
By keeping our application architecture as simple as possible, we can spend our complexity (and headcount) budget in places where there's complexity that it benefits our business to take on.
key_insight: Simple architectures remain undervalued in an industry that fetishizes complexity, even though they enable most companies to scale profitably while keeping engineering focused on delivering user value rather than managing self-imposed architectural overhead.
